title: 1,444 Zombie Processes, 91GB RAM: Why Playwright Always Leaks and How to Build an Automated Reaper
summary: After 12 hours of running 81 parallel Playwright visual test runners, we found 1,444 orphaned Chromium processes consuming 91GB of memory. This isn't about how to kill processes—it's about why Playwright inevitably leaks, why SIGTERM can't kill Chrome, and how an AI agent became our cron watchdog for 72 hours straight.
body:
# 1,444 Zombie Processes, 91GB RAM: Why Playwright Always Leaks and How to Build an Automated Reaper

## Finding the Bodies

At midnight, I asked Claude Code to check for any stray browser processes on the machine.

The first thing it said back was "Holy shit."

```
Total processes: 1,444
Total memory usage: ~91 GB
Total CPU usage: 638%
Playwright sessions: ~140+ independent browser sessions
```

On a single MacBook, 1,444 Chromium processes were sitting quietly, consuming 91GB of memory, doing absolutely nothing. They were child processes of Playwright headless browsers—GPU helpers, Renderers, Utilities—adopted by macOS's launchd after their test runners exited, all with PPID 1.

Classic orphan process scenario. But the scale was absurd.

## Profiling the Culprit

We quickly traced the source: **81 parallel Playwright visual regression test runners**, all running `--update-snapshots`. The timeline stretched from 11 AM to midnight, over 12 hours of continuous execution.

| Time Period | Active Runners |
|-------------|----------------|
| 11:29AM - 11:39AM | 3 |
| 2:48PM - 3:21PM | 3 |
| 4:30PM - 5:57PM | 9 |
| 7:17PM - 8:54PM | 18 |
| 8:57PM - 11:36PM | 34 |

Like some kind of breeding experiment—new test batches kept spawning while old ones were still running. Each runner spawns a Chrome main process, Chrome forks 4-6 child processes. 81 x 5 = 400+ processes running simultaneously, plus historical stragglers, easily breaking 1,000.

## First Cut: Cleaning Up Old Corpses

We started by clearing obviously stale processes. The user chose "kill only old ones"—preserving active runners still updating snapshots.

The first round eliminated 36 old Playwright node processes and 101 old Chromium children. But we immediately hit a problem: **killing the parent process doesn't kill the children**.

```bash
kill -TERM <playwright-pid>  # Parent dies
# But its Chrome children live on! PPID becomes 1
```

We tried macOS process group killing:

```bash
kill -TERM -- -$(pgid)  # exit 144, unreliable
```

Dead end.

## Why SIGTERM Can't Kill Chrome

This is the most fascinating technical detail in the whole story.

Chrome uses multi-process architecture. The main process manages child processes (GPU process, Renderer process, Utility process, etc.) through IPC. These child processes **double-fork to escape the original process group** after startup—this is Chrome's design decision for crash isolation.

More critically: **Chrome child processes deliberately ignore SIGTERM**. This isn't a bug, it's a feature. Chrome wants the main process to coordinate shutdown through IPC, not be interrupted by external signals.

So the cleanup chain breaks at two points:

1. `kill -TERM` -> Chrome children ignore it
2. Process group kill -> Children are no longer in the original process group

The only way out: **individual PID `kill -9` (SIGKILL)**. SIGKILL can't be caught, can't be ignored—the kernel directly reclaims resources.

## Process Age Detection: etime is the Gold Standard

Cleanup strategy decided—filter by process age, kill -9 anything older than 2 hours. But how to determine "how long has this process been running"?

First instinct was ps start time (column $9):

```
12:08AM   # Midnight? Or noon?
11:40AM   # Today or yesterday?
```

LSTART is more precise but formatted as `Mon Apr 13 23:31:25 2026`—cross-day comparisons require manual calculation. Plus AM/PM format behaves inconsistently across locales.

The right answer is `ps -eo pid,etime,command` **elapsed time** field:

```
02-20:18:38   # 2 days 20 hours 18 minutes 38 seconds
   03:14:22   # 3 hours 14 minutes 22 seconds
      14:22   # 14 minutes 22 seconds
```

Format is DD-HH:MM:SS, directly telling you "how long this process has been alive." Parsing is straightforward:

```bash
awk '{split($2,p,"[-:]"); n=length(p);
  if(n==2) m=p[1];           # MM:SS
  else if(n==3) m=p[1]*60+p[2];    # HH:MM:SS
  else if(n==4) m=p[1]*1440+p[2]*60+p[3]; # DD-HH:MM:SS
  if(m>=120) print $1}'  # PIDs older than 120 minutes
```

This pattern works for any "cleanup by process age" scenario, not limited to Playwright.

## Complete Cleanup Command

The final stable one-liner:

```bash
OLD_PIDS=$(ps -eo pid,etime,command \
  | grep -E '(playwright.*process.js|@playwright/test/cli.js|Google Chrome for Testing)' \
  | grep -v grep \
  | awk '{split($2,p,"[-:]");n=length(p);
    if(n==2)m=p[1];else if(n==3)m=p[1]*60+p[2];
    else if(n==4)m=p[1]*1440+p[2]*60+p[3];
    if(m>=120)print $1}')
COUNT=$(echo "$OLD_PIDS" | grep -c . 2>/dev/null || echo 0)
[ "$COUNT" -gt 0 ] && echo "$OLD_PIDS" | xargs kill -9 2>/dev/null \
  && echo "Killed $COUNT" || echo "None found"
ps aux | grep -E '(playwright|Chrome for Testing)' | grep -v grep \
  | awk '{c++;s+=$6}END{printf "Remaining: %d procs, ~%.0fMB\n",c,s/1024}'
```

Four steps: filter -> count -> kill -> report remaining. Clean and efficient.

## Cron Watchdog: Making an AI Agent the Guardian

Cleaning once manually wasn't enough—test runners were still active, new orphan processes kept spawning. We needed automation.

Claude Code has built-in cron scheduling capability. We set up an hourly watchdog running at :07 minutes:

```
Cron: 7 * * * *
Task: Clean browser test processes older than 2 hours
TTL: 7 days auto-expire, session-only
```

This watchdog ran continuously for **72 hours**, faithfully executing cleanup every hour. The data trends were fascinating:

| Phase | Time Span | Kill/Hour | Remaining Procs | Remaining Memory |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|------------------|
| Steady State | First 24h | ~102 | ~102 | ~8GB |
| Burst Phase | Day 2 | ~102 | 194->478->714 | 14->30->43GB |
| High Steady | Day 2-3 | ~102 | 714 | 43->40GB |

Steady state makes sense: ~102 new orphans generated per hour, ~102 cleaned per hour, balanced.

What happened during the burst phase? A new wave of test runners launched, spawning far faster than cleanup could handle. Remaining processes jumped from 102 to 714, memory from 8GB to 43GB. But the watchdog kept providing a backstop, preventing infinite growth.

Most interesting was Day 3's discovery: **all 714 processes had PPID 1, but test runner count was 0**. All runners were dead, all Chrome children were alive. Textbook orphan process case.

## Root Cause: Why Playwright Inevitably Leaks

Playwright controls Chrome through Chrome DevTools Protocol (CDP) pipe (`--remote-debugging-pipe`). The normal shutdown path is:

```
Playwright -> Browser.close() -> Chrome main -> IPC -> child processes exit
```

This chain breaks under three conditions:

**1. Abnormal Termination**

When test runners are OOM killed, SIGKILLed, or crash directly, the CDP pipe breaks. `Browser.close()` never gets a chance. Playwright registers `process.on('exit')` cleanup hooks, but **SIGKILL doesn't trigger exit handlers**.

**2. Chrome Child Processes Escape Process Group**

Chrome's child processes double-fork to escape the original process group. Even if the main Chrome process exits, GPU/Renderer helpers exist independently, PPID directly becomes 1.

**3. SIGTERM Immunity**

As explained earlier. Chrome child processes are immune to SIGTERM, only SIGKILL can terminate them.

In my scenario, 81 parallel workers amplified the problem: memory pressure -> macOS starts OOM killing some runners -> runners die but Chrome stays -> next round spawns new ones -> accumulation cycle.

**Conclusion: External watchdog is the only reliable solution.** Playwright's own cleanup is a design limitation, not a bug.

## Mitigation Strategies

1. **Limit Parallelism**: `--workers=8` instead of default CPU core count. 81 workers is suicidal load
2. **Set Timeouts**: After test timeout, Playwright gets a chance to walk the normal cleanup path
3. **Explicit `browser.close()`**: Manual close in afterAll hooks, reducing orphan probability
4. **External Watchdog**: Must-have. The etime + kill -9 solution above, as a cron job or CI post-step

But orphans **can never be reduced to zero**. As long as there's possibility of OOM, crash, timeout force-kill, there will be orphans. Accept this reality, then automate the reaping.

## Reflection

This session spanned 3 days. Most of the time was an AI agent mechanically executing hourly ps | grep | awk | kill cycles. In a sense, this is exactly what agents are best suited for—repetitive, mechanical, continuous-attention-but-no-creativity operational tasks.

But it also exposed a blind spot in the agent pattern: cron watchdogs are session-only, gone when Claude exits. Real production environments need systemd timers or launchd plists—persistent solutions not dependent on any specific session.

If you're also running large-scale visual tests with Playwright, remember two things:

1. **Orphans are inevitable, not accidents**
2. **Watchdog isn't nice-to-have, it's must-have**

A 91GB lesson is lesson enough.
